ShareCommentary:
I've been refinishing furniture lately. I picked up a piece at a garage sale and needed to strip the old varnish before I could do anything with it."
""
"I tried six different approaches. Scrapers, heat guns, chemical strippers of varying toxicity. Some worked a little. Most didn't. I spent way more time on this than I expected."
""
"When I finally found the right method, it worked almost immediately with barely any effort."
""
"That's the part that frustrated me. Not the struggle, the fact that the answer felt so obvious once I had it. Like I should have known. Looking back, the signs were there. I just didn't see them until I did."
""
"IT people know this feeling."
""
"The source of an outage is a mystery right up until the moment it isn't. The misconfiguration looks invisible until someone spots it, and then you can't unsee it. You've been staring at the same screen for an hour and a colleague glances over and finds it in thirty seconds."
""
"It's not a knowledge problem. It's a perspective problem. Sometimes you're too close to it, or too deep in one approach, to see what's actually in front of you."
""
"I don't have a clean answer for how to fix that. But I'm curious if you do."
""
"How do you break out of the tunnel when you're deep in a problem you can't solve?

Here's a pattern I've seen play out more than once."
""
"An Architect designs something: a network component, an application structure, a process. It gets handed to Engineers to implement. Then Operations picks it up and supports it day to day."
""
"And that works fine. Until something changes."
""
"Maybe a problem surfaces during implementation. Maybe a new solution comes in and the old one has to adapt. Either way, something in the system is different now than what the original documentation described."
""
"So who updates the docs?"
""
"The quick answer is everyone. Which in practice means no one."
""
"The change gets made. People get busy. The documentation sits there, a record of how things used to work. The next person who needs it doesn't know that yet."
""
"It's not that people don't care. It's just what you're currently working on always feels more important than updating something you're not actively using. So it gets assumed. Someone else will get to it. They don't."
""
"What I've started doing with my teams is getting ahead of it. Who owns this application? This process? What does that ownership actually mean day to day?"
""
"Put a name next to it. One name. Not a team, not a role. A person."
""
"Assumed ownership isn't ownership. It's just a gap waiting to be found."
""
"What do you do to keep ownership from falling through the cracks?

Re-posting for visibility. I worked with Vamshi at HDR and was always amazed at his troubleshooting and documentation skills. He is an amazing Network Engineer. If you're looking for a Network Engineer, Vamshi's the one.

I built two pieces of furniture around the same time. One for me, one for my daughter."
""
"The one for me turned out well. There's a joint I wasn't completely happy with but I let it go. I look at it sometimes and think it could've been better. Good enough though. It was just for me."
""
"The one for my daughter was different. I double checked every cut. Made sure nothing shifted during the glue up. Sanded everything smooth. Watched the finish dry. Fixed anything that didn't look right before moving on."
""
"Same tools. Same shop. Very different standard."
""
"I didn't make a conscious decision to care more about one than the other. It just happened. Knowing she'd have it in her own home someday, that she'd use it, that it came from me - that changed how I worked."
""
"I think about that in IT too. It's easy to let "good enough" become the default when you're not thinking about the person on the other end. The user who can't do their job because the system is slow. The team member who inherited a process nobody documented properly. The customer who just wants the thing to work."
""
"Knowing who something is for has a way of raising your standard without you even having to think about it."
""
"Who are you building for right now?

Sharing for visibility. I had the honor of working with Paresh at HDR. He is incredibly smart and driven. I highly encourage anyone that is looking to hire a talented Network Engineer to take some time to talk to him. You will be blown away!

We are hiring a Network Engineer at Creighton University."
""
"We are growing our team and building out capabilities around some genuinely interesting technology. If you want to work on ACI, ISE, Infoblox, and Palo Alto as a core part of your day job, this is worth a look."
""
"You will work on enterprise-grade infrastructure and be expected to own your work."
""
"Omaha-based. Good people. A university that actually cares about its staff."
""
"If that sounds like a fit, apply below. If you know someone who would be great at this, tag them.

A friend told me recently about a school project his daughter had worked on. She had to describe what her parents did for work."
""
"She wrote that her dad works with computers. And clowns. But he doesn’t work for the circus."
""
"I laughed for about two minutes straight."
""
"He probably said something offhand at dinner one night about a frustrating coworker. Maybe called them a clown. Didn’t think twice about it. He was just venting."
""
"But his daughter heard it, filed it away, and it became part of her understanding of what he does for a living."
""
"We do this at work too. The offhand comment in the hallway. The joke in a meeting that wasn’t really a joke. The frustrated remark you made to one person that made its way to three others by the end of the day."
""
"We think we’re just talking. Other people think we’re telling them something."
""
"I’ve said things I thought were throwaway observations that turned out to matter a lot to the person who heard them. In both directions - things that landed better than I expected and things I wish I could take back."
""
"The words you say casually are still words. People hear them, remember them, and sometimes repeat them to their eighth grade class."
""
"What’s the most unintentionally revealing thing you’ve ever said at work?

A while back I decided to refinish some furniture. I’d never done it before. I spent more time researching the right way to do it than I did actually doing it."
""
"What stripper should I use? What grit sandpaper? What finish? In what order? What if I chose wrong and ruined it?"
""
"I was completely paralyzed."
""
"Eventually I had to put the YouTube videos down and pick up the tools. So I did. And yes, I got partway through and had to stop, strip everything back down, and start over. The end result didn’t look anything like what I had pictured when I started."
""
"It sold 1 day after I listed it. "
""
"Here’s what I figured out: I was treating “right” like it was a fixed point I could find if I just researched long enough. It isn’t. "
"Especially when you’re doing something for the first time. Right is something you work your way toward, not something you find before you start."
""
"The piece taught me more than any video did. The restart taught me even more."
""
"In IT work there’s always a reason to wait until you know more. More requirements, more testing, more planning. Sometimes that’s legitimate. Sometimes it’s just the fear of an imperfect result dressed up as due diligence."
""
"Progress usually looks like a restart somewhere in the middle.

I had someone on my team whose job was managing WAN circuits. Ordering them, tracking them, making sure the right capacity was in the right place. That was her lane, and she was good at it."
""
"A ticket came in one day about a VPN client issue that had stumped everyone who looked at it. Not a WAN problem. Not her area. She wasn’t assigned to it."
""
"She overheard people talking about it, looked into it on her own, and figured it out in about ten minutes."
""
"I remember thinking about how that almost didn’t happen. Nothing in her role suggested she’d have the answer. If she had stayed inside the lines of what her job description said she was responsible for, that ticket would have stayed open a lot longer."
""
"We do this constantly with the people around us. We see the role and stop looking at the person. The title becomes a ceiling on what we think someone is capable of contributing."
""
"The most capable teams I’ve been part of weren’t the ones with the best org charts. They were the ones where people were given room to bring more than their job description to the table."
""
"You probably have someone like that right now. The question is whether you’re giving them a chance to show it.

There are two ways to approach a cut in the shop."
""
"The first is cutting to a line. You measure, mark it, and hit the mark. Precise, repeatable, confident. This is how most of the work gets done."
""
"The second is cutting to fit. You get close, test it, shave a little more, test it again. You’re not chasing a number on a tape measure. You’re chasing the result."
""
"Knowing which one the situation calls for is the real skill."
""
"Early on I defaulted to cutting to a line every time. If the measurement was right, the piece would be right. Except wood moves. Walls aren’t plumb. Floors aren’t level. Sometimes the line is right and the fit is still wrong."
""
"The plan is always built on assumptions. Some of those assumptions don’t survive contact with the actual material."
""
"IT work is the same way. You can spec a project carefully, document every requirement, build exactly what was asked for. And still find yourself in a room where the thing doesn’t quite fit the environment it was built for."
""
"The plan matters. So does knowing when to put the tape measure down and start working toward the fit.

When you start a new job, everyone is willing to answer your questions. Where’s this, how does that work, who do I talk to about this? People are generous with the formal version of how things operate."
""
"That’s helpful. But it’s not the most useful thing."
""
"Start paying attention to the stuff nobody says in response to a question. The comments made under people’s breath after a meeting ends. The slight pause before they answer something that should have been simple. The way two people glance at each other when a certain topic comes up."
""
"That’s where the real information lives."
""
"Every team has a stated way of doing things and an actual way of doing things. The gap between those two is what you actually need to understand. You can’t close that gap by asking about it directly. Most of the time the people living inside it don’t even see it clearly themselves."
""
"You close it by listening to what surfaces without being prompted."
""
"The questions you ask in a new job matter. But the answers you weren’t looking for are usually the ones worth writing down."
""
"What do you pay attention to when you’re learning a new environment?

I was fitting a drawer in the shop a few weeks ago. One corner had a slight gap, so I trimmed the side down to close it."
""
"It closed. But now the opposite corner didn’t sit right. So I adjusted that. Then the drawer face was off. Then the reveal was uneven."
""
"I spent an hour fixing a problem I had already fixed."
""
"The mistake wasn’t the first adjustment. The mistake was thinking the first adjustment only affected one thing."
""
"A drawer isn’t a single piece. It’s a set of relationships. Change one dimension and you’ve changed all of them, whether you meant to or not. The experienced woodworker knows to stop, look at the whole thing, and understand what’s actually connected before reaching for the saw."
""
"IT systems work the same way. A quick fix to one component shifts load somewhere else. A configuration change resolves the alert and creates a new one three layers down. A process improvement in one team creates a bottleneck in another."
""
"The problem usually isn’t that people make changes. It’s that they make changes while only looking at one piece."
""
"Before you trim something down, know what it’s connected to.

There’s a version of work stress that isn’t about the actual problem. It’s about every possible version of the problem that hasn’t happened yet."
""
"What if this change breaks something? What if the rollout doesn’t go as planned? What if I’m missing something and it blows up on a Friday afternoon?"
""
"I’ve spent a lot of time in that loop over the years. And the hard part is that none of those questions are unreasonable. They come from experience. Something has gone wrong before, and your brain is just trying to protect you."
""
"But at some point the questions stop being useful and start being noise."
""
"A while back someone gave me a simple reframe and it actually stuck. Change “what if” to “even if.”"
""
"What if this goes wrong becomes even if this goes wrong, we can recover it. What if they push back becomes even if they push back, here’s how I’ll handle it. What if I’m missing something becomes even if I’m missing something, I’ll find it and fix it."
""
"It’s not about pretending problems won’t happen. It’s about reminding yourself that you can handle them when they do."
""
"That shift doesn’t eliminate the stress. But it moves you from spinning to planning.

There’s a moment in the shop before any work starts that I’ve come to appreciate. A new plank of wood, just sitting there. Straight, uncut, full of possibility."
""
"It could become almost anything. A cabinet, a shelf, a tabletop. The grain hasn’t told you what it wants to be yet. Neither have you."
""
"The moment you make the first cut, that changes. You’ve committed to something. The other possibilities don’t disappear, but they narrow. Every cut after that narrows them a little more."
""
"I started a new job this week and it feels exactly like that."
""
"A new place hasn’t formed an opinion of you yet. You haven’t made the cuts that define you there. The relationships, the reputation, the habits people will associate with your name — none of that exists yet. It’s just a clean plank."
""
"That’s not a reason to be paralyzed by the possibilities. You still have to pick something up and start working."
""
"But it’s worth standing there for a second before you do. Thinking about what you actually want to build."
""
"The wood doesn’t stay uncut forever. Neither does the opportunity.

I started a new job this week. But before I did, my wife and I took a few days and got out of town."
""
"We read books. Ate at places we’d never been. Walked through small shops without any agenda. I didn’t even bring a laptop. Thought about the new job less than I expected to."
""
"It wasn’t avoidance. It was a deliberate gap between one chapter and the next."
""
"I’ve made the mistake before of rolling straight from one job into the next with no space in between. You show up on day one still carrying the last place. The unfinished frustrations, the habits that were built for a different environment, the version of yourself that was shaped by two years of a specific team and culture."
""
"That stuff doesn’t clear out on its own. You have to give it somewhere to go."
""
"A few days away did that. I came back with a clearer head than I would have had otherwise. Ready to actually pay attention instead of just showing up."
""
"The transition is part of the job. Most of us skip it entirely."
""
"What do you do to reset between big professional changes?

I cut every piece for a small cabinet last weekend. Drew up the cut list, measured twice, ran everything through the saw. Felt good about it."
""
"When I went to dry fit the drawers, nothing lined up. I stood there for a minute just staring at it. Went back to the cut list and found it immediately. I had written down the final dimension for the drawer sides but never made that last cut. The pieces were close, just not right."
""
"So I pulled the tools back out and took them down to size. Twenty minutes of work I hadn’t planned on."
""
"The thing is, I caught it during a dry fit. No glue, no fasteners, nothing permanent. The mistake was real, but the cost was low."
""
"That’s what a dry fit is for. Not to confirm everything went perfectly. To find out what didn’t before it’s too late to fix it."
""
"I think about that in IT work too. A staging environment, a change window test, a tabletop walkthrough before a cutover. These aren’t extra steps. They’re the part of the process that makes the rest of it recoverable."
""
"A plan that looks complete on paper still has gaps. The dry fit is how you find them.

I watched my son’s track meet last week and kept thinking about how brave the kids running hurdles were."
""
"Aren’t they afraid of falling? Then one of them caught his toe on a hurdle and went down hard. But instead of sprawling out, he tucked into a forward roll and came up on his feet, barely breaking stride."
""
"He wasn’t unafraid of falling. He’d learned how to fall."
""
"That stuck with me because it’s the same thing we try to do with systems."
""
"All systems fail eventually. The question isn’t if, it’s when and how. A server goes down. A circuit drops. A patch breaks something you didn’t expect it to break."
""
"The teams that handle it well aren’t the ones who never fall. They’re the ones who’ve thought through how they’ll fall and what they’ll do when it happens."
""
"We spend a lot of time designing systems to stay up. But we don’t always spend enough time designing how they’ll fail. Where the dependencies are. What breaks first. What stays online when something else goes down."
""
"What we try to do now is ask one extra question during design. When this fails, what happens next? Not if. When."
""
"Because knowing how to fall is just as important as knowing how to run."
""
"How does your team design for failure, not just uptime?

A chisel stays sharp longer if you hone it between uses."
""
"Not a full sharpening. Just a few passes on a strop to realign the edge. Takes 30 seconds. But I skip it all the time because I’m in the middle of a project and stopping feels like it slows me down."
""
"So I keep cutting with a chisel that’s getting duller with every pass. The cuts take more effort, the edges get rougher, and eventually I have to stop anyway to do a full resharpening that takes 10 minutes instead of 30 seconds."
""
"I lose more time avoiding the small maintenance than I would have spent just doing it."
""
"I see this pattern in IT constantly."
""
"We skip the weekly check-in because everyone’s busy. We don’t update documentation after a change because we’ll do it later. We put off patching a server because the maintenance window feels inconvenient."
""
"Small maintenance feels like it’s getting in the way of real work. But skipping it just means the real work gets harder until something forces you to stop and fix what you’ve been ignoring."
""
"The pull to keep moving is strong, especially when you’ve got momentum."
""
"The habit I’m trying to build is pausing for the small maintenance before it turns into big maintenance. 30 seconds now or 10 minutes later."
""
"What’s one piece of small maintenance your team actually makes time for instead of putting off?

We had the same outage twice in a year because we didn’t follow through on the lesson the first time."
""
"A year ago, a chiller went down at one of our colo data centers. Storage overheated and took down our monitoring server. No alerts went out because the thing that sends alerts was offline."
""
"The chiller was fixed. We wrote down what was impacted. We talked about diversifying monitoring to other data centers so a failure in one location wouldn’t blind us everywhere."
""
"And then we moved on to the next thing."
""
"Last month, the chiller went down again. Same data center. Same cascade. Same result. No alerts because the monitoring server was sitting in the room that overheated."
""
"The first time was bad luck. The second time was on us."
""
"I know how it happened. After an outage, you fix what broke and you’re exhausted. Writing up lessons learned feels like the finish line. But the writeup isn’t the work. The follow-through is."
""
"What we do now is treat post-incident action items like any other project. They get assigned, they get scheduled, and someone owns making sure they actually happen."
""
"Because if you don’t change anything after an incident, you didn’t learn anything. You just survived it."
""
"How does your team make sure the lessons from an outage actually turn into changes instead of just turning into documentation?

A drill press is only accurate if the table is level."
""
"I was drilling holes for shelf pins last weekend and didn’t check the table before I started. It looked flat, the bit was sharp, and I had a dozen holes to drill. I lined up the first one, pulled the lever, and kept moving."
""
"Five holes in I realized they weren’t straight. They angled slightly to one side. The table had shifted maybe two degrees, just enough that every hole was off."
""
"I had to plug them all and start over. The drill did exactly what I set it up to do. The setup was the problem."
""
"I see this happen in IT when we build on top of something that isn’t square."
""
"We roll out a new policy but the user list it’s based on is six months out of date. We automate a workflow but the process it’s automating was never standardized. We deploy monitoring but the baselines we’re measuring against were never validated."
"The tool works. The execution is fine. But if the foundation is tilted, everything that follows will be tilted too."
""
"I still skip this step sometimes. It feels faster to just start drilling than to stop and check the setup first."
""
"The habit I’m building is simple. Before I run the process, I check the table. Two minutes to confirm the foundation is level saves an hour of rework later."
""
"What’s one place where your team checks the foundation before running the tool?

I had to tell a department head last month that we couldn’t deliver what they were asking for on the timeline they wanted."
""
"They needed a new site stood up with full redundancy in three weeks. The timeline was tied to a lease signing and they’d already committed to the date with their leadership."
""
"We could have said yes. We could have cut corners, skipped testing, and pushed something out the door. But it wouldn’t have been stable, and when it broke, we’d own that too."
""
"So I said no. Not to the project, but to the timeline. I explained what we could deliver in three weeks and what we’d need another month for if they wanted it done right."
""
"It was uncomfortable. They weren’t happy. But they adjusted the lease timeline and we delivered something that actually worked."
""
"Saying no up front and being clear about what we could actually deliver took pressure off the team. They had a timeline they could meet instead of one they were set up to miss."
""
"I’ve learned this the hard way. Saying yes when you should say no doesn’t make you helpful. It makes you the person who overpromises and underdelivers."
""
"What we try to do now is be clear about what’s possible and what’s not before we commit. It’s easier to negotiate a realistic timeline up front than to explain why you missed an unrealistic one later."
""
"Saying no doesn’t mean being a blocker. It means being honest about what you can actually deliver."
""
"How does your team handle requests that aren’t realistic without just becoming the team that always says no?

The cuts were perfect. The glue held. And I still had to take the whole thing apart because I wasn't paying attention when I assembled it."
""
"I was gluing leg supports and had everything laid out. The cuts were right, the sanding was done, and I was ready to assemble. I worked through each joint carefully, clamped everything tight, and let it cure."
"When I slid the two pieces together, one set of legs pointed down. The other set pointed up."
""
"I'd glued one of the braces on backwards. Not because I didn't know which way it should go. Because I wasn't paying attention when it mattered."
"The wood was fine. The joinery was solid. But I built it wrong because I was on autopilot."
""
"I see this happen in IT more than I want to admit."
"We provision a new account and copy the wrong template. We configure a device and flip a setting without reading what it actually does. We run a script in production that we meant to test in staging first."
""
"The steps are right. The process is clear. But we're moving fast or distracted or both, and we don't catch the mistake until it's already done."
"I still do this. The work feels routine so your brain checks out, and that's exactly when you make the mistake that costs you time."
""
"The habit I'm trying to build is slowing down for two seconds before the irreversible step. Glue, deploy, delete, send. Just long enough to confirm I'm doing what I think I'm doing."
"What does your team do to catch mistakes that happen when the work feels routine?

The first thing I do when I start with a new team is listen for some time before I change anything."
""
"I took over a network team a few years ago and the instinct was strong to start fixing things right away. There were obvious gaps. Processes that didn’t make sense. Tools that seemed outdated."
""
"But I didn’t know the context yet. I didn’t know why things were the way they were or what had already been tried."
""
"So I spent the first few weeks in one-on-ones and sitting in on their daily work. I asked questions. I watched how they handled tickets, how they communicated during issues, what frustrated them."
""
"By the end of that time I had a much clearer picture. Some of the things I thought were problems were actually workarounds for constraints I didn’t know existed. And some of the real problems weren’t the ones I would have guessed."
""
"The changes we made after that landed better because the team knew I’d listened first."
""
"I still have to remind myself of this. When you’re new or when you’re trying to prove yourself, the pressure to move fast is real. But rushing past the listening part usually means you fix the wrong things."
""
"The habit I keep coming back to is simple. Some time asking questions before jumping to answers."
""
"When you join a new team, how do you figure out what actually needs to change versus what just looks broken from the outside?

A miter saw cuts fast, but only if the fence is square."
""
"I was cutting trim pieces last weekend for a small project. Ten cuts, all at 45 degrees, all supposed to match. I didn’t check the fence before I started. It looked square, and I wanted to get moving."
"By the fifth piece I could see the angles weren’t lining up. I checked the fence. It was off by half a degree. Not much, but enough that nothing fit together cleanly."
""
"I had to recut everything. The saw did exactly what I told it to. The setup was the problem."
"I see this happen in IT when we’re moving fast."
"We deploy a new tool without setting the baseline config first. We onboard a new team member using last quarter’s process because no one updated the documentation. We build automation on top of a workflow that was never standardized to begin with."
""
"The work gets done quickly. But if the foundation isn’t square, the output won’t be either. And you won’t notice until you try to put the pieces together."
""
"I still rush the setup sometimes. It’s hard to slow down when you just want to start cutting."
""
"The habit I’m working on is checking the baseline before I run the process. Takes two minutes. Saves having to redo the work later."
""
"What’s one place where your team checks the setup before running the process?

Three active firewall rules. No documentation. No owner. No idea what would break if we removed them."
""
"We found them last month during a routine audit. They were there. They were active. But no tickets referenced them, no documentation covered them, and the person who added them had left the team two years ago."
""
"We spent half a day tracking down what they were for before we felt safe enough to touch them. Turns out two were for a project that had been decommissioned. The third was still needed, but we only figured that out by spending even more time on research. "
""
"Exceptions happen. A temporary rule for a vendor demo. A one-off port opening for a contractor. A quick bypass to unblock someone during an outage."
""
"The problem isn’t that we make exceptions. The problem is we don’t document them the same way we document everything else."
"I’ve been guilty of this more times than I want to admit. You make the change, it works, and you move on. Documenting it feels like extra work when the fire is out."
""
"What we do now is simple. Every exception gets a ticket and a note in the change log with an expiration date. Even if it’s supposed to be temporary. Especially if it’s supposed to be temporary."
""
"Because undocumented exceptions don’t stay exceptions. They become landmines."
"How does your team make sure temporary fixes don’t turn into permanent mysteries?

Sandpaper only works if you let it do the work."
""
"I was smoothing a tabletop last weekend and got impatient. The grain was rough and I wanted it done, so I started pressing harder. More pressure, faster results, right?"
""
"Wrong. The paper clogged, the surface got uneven, and I ended up with burn marks I had to sand out all over again."
""
"Sandpaper works by cutting a little bit at a time. When you force it, you just create more problems."
""
"I see this pattern show up in IT all the time."
""
"A project is behind so we add more people mid-stream. A process isn't working so we add more steps instead of fixing the broken ones. A system is slow so we throw more resources at it without understanding why it's struggling."
""
"It feels productive. But pressure without clarity just creates friction."
"I've done this more times than I want to admit. The instinct when something isn't moving fast enough is to push harder. But that's usually when you need to back off and check what's actually happening."
""
"What we try to do now is pause and ask one question when progress stalls. Are we stuck because we need more force, or because something in the approach isn't working?"
""
"How does your team tell the difference between needing to push harder and needing to change the approach?

I spent the afternoon on Saturday volunteering with my family at a charity that we’ve been a part of for the last 24 years. "
""
"ReRUNS R Fun is the nation’s largest non-profit consignment sale for children, teens, and women. Located in LaVista, Nebraska, ReRUNS hosts resale events twice a year. All profits from the sales events are donated to charities in our community and around the world."
""
"Back when we were scrounging in the couch cushions looking for loose change so we could afford groceries, ReRUNS helped us afford to buy clothes and toys for our children. My wife has worked at every sale and has made so many friends there. From an unwilling participant, I now look forward to the time that I get to give back and help those that need things when couch nickles don’t buy what they used to. "
""
"One of our tasks this weekend was to organize all of the board games, which we have been doing for the last several sales. "
""
"How does this relate to my previous posts about networking and business? It doesn’t. Volunteering is just good for the soul.

The first cut on a table saw is the one that matters most."
""
"Not because it’s the hardest. Because it sets the reference for every cut that follows."
""
"I was ripping boards for a bookshelf a few weeks ago and rushed the first pass. The fence was close enough, the blade looked square, and I wanted to get moving. Three boards in I noticed they weren’t lining up right. I checked the fence. It was off by an eighth of an inch."
""
"Every board I’d cut was wrong by the same amount. I had to start over."
""
"I think about that when we’re setting up new systems or processes at work."
""
"The first user you provision sets the pattern for how accounts get created. The first ticket template becomes the standard everyone copies. The first time you let a workaround slide, it becomes the way things are done."
"If the reference is off at the start, everything that follows will be off too. And you won’t notice until you’re three boards in and nothing lines up."
""
"I still rush this sometimes. It’s hard to slow down at the beginning when you just want to get things moving."
""
"The habit I’m building is checking the setup twice before I make the first cut. It takes an extra two minutes. It saves hours later."
""
"What’s one place where your team sets the reference, and how do you make sure it’s right from the start?

The carrier delayed the circuit by three weeks. We delayed telling anyone by two. Guess which one caused more problems."
""
"We had a new office coming online and the carrier pushed the install date back three weeks. Not ideal, but it happens. Circuits get delayed. Permitting runs long. Someone misses a window."
""
"The real issue was that we didn’t tell the business unit until five days before the original go-live date. They had people scheduled to move in, equipment ordered, onboarding planned. And suddenly they had to scramble to set everyone up for WFH while we waited for the line to get installed."
""
"The delay cost us three weeks. The late communication cost us trust."
"I’ve learned this the hard way. When something slips, the instinct is to wait until you have a fix before you say anything. You don’t want to sound the alarm if it might still work out. But that leaves everyone else without time to adapt."
""
"What we do now is simple. If a timeline is at risk, we say it as soon as we know. Even if we don’t have the solution yet. Even if it might still land on time."
""
"Bad news early is better than bad news late. The business can work with three weeks of notice. They can’t work with five days."
""
"How does your team decide when to escalate a delay versus when to wait and see if you can fix it first?

You can't rush glue."
""
"I learned that trying to assemble a small cabinet last month. I had the joints cut, everything fit tight, and I was ready to move on to the next step. So I clamped it up, gave it maybe 40 minutes, and started working the face flush with a hand plane."
""
"The joint opened. Not much, but enough that I couldn't fix it without taking the whole thing apart and starting over."
""
"The glue needed an hour. I gave it 40 minutes because I wanted to keep moving."
""
"I see the same thing happen with changes in IT."
""
"We push a patch because the window is closing and we want it done. We move someone into a new role before the handoff is really finished because we need the seat filled. We say a process is ready because we tested it once and it mostly worked."
""
"It feels like progress. But if the foundation isn't set, you end up going backward."
""
"I still catch myself doing this. The pull to keep moving is strong, especially when you've got momentum or pressure or both."
""
"The habit I'm trying to build is asking one question before I move on. Is this actually done, or does it just feel done because I'm tired of waiting?"
""
"What helps you know when something is ready versus when you just want it to be ready?

We lost a few hours last week because no one wanted to say "I don't know.""
""
"A ticket came in about slowness in one of our office networks. The engineer who picked it up ran through the standard checks and then spent an hour poking around, unsuccessfully, trying to find the root cause. They put the ticket on hold to get back to it after lunch."
""
"While they were out, a second engineer took a look. After another hour they threw up their hands and asked a senior engineer for help."
""
"The senior engineer pointed out a misconfiguration that wasn't obvious unless you knew what to look for."
""
"The fix took five minutes once it was pointed out."
""
"I get it. No one wants to be the person who doesn't have the answer. Especially when you're newer or when the team is busy. But "I don't know" followed by "let me pull someone in" is faster than pretending you do."
""
"What we changed is small. If a ticket sits with you for more than 30 minutes without progress, you say something. You don't have to solve it. You just have to stop it from sitting there."
""
"It's not about knowing everything. It's about not letting a problem age while you figure it out alone."
""
"How does your team make it safe to say "I need another set of eyes on this"?

A sharp chisel does exactly what you ask it to do. That's the problem."
""
"I was trimming a mortise last weekend, moving fast, confident the angle was right. One extra pass and I had a gap I couldn't fix. The chisel didn't make a mistake. I did. And sharp tools don't forgive sloppy thinking the way dull ones do."
""
"I think about that in IT a lot."
""
"When your team is good, when the systems are tuned and the access is wide, things move fast. A change gets pushed without a second set of eyes. A firewall rule gets added "just temporarily." An account stays active two weeks after someone leaves because nobody owns the offboarding process."
""
"Sharp teams cut fast. That's mostly a good thing. But speed without a moment of pause is how small decisions become expensive ones."
""
"I've learned this the hard way, usually right after something went sideways on a Friday afternoon."
""
"The habit we built is simple. We explain every change in our Change Board before it runs. Having to talk about it out loud exposes more than you'd think, not because others see it, but because it makes you think about it differently."
""
"What does your team do to slow down just enough before a sharp tool does exactly what you told it to?

Sometimes the hardest decision is not what to do."
""
"It’s when to do it."
""
"We had something the team really wanted to implement. It was a good idea. It would have improved things. And honestly, we were excited about it."
""
"But when we looked at timing and risk, we realized we were trying to squeeze it into a week that already had too many moving parts."
""
"If we pushed it through, we probably could have made it work."
""
"But we also would have been doing it in a rushed, tired state. And that’s when small mistakes turn into bigger problems."
""
"So we made the call to wait."
""
"Not because the idea was bad."
"Not because we didn’t care."
"But because we wanted to do it well."
""
"We wrote down what “ready” needed to look like:"
"What had to be tested."
"What dependencies had to be confirmed."
"What communications needed to go out."
"What window made sense."
""
"That decision wasn’t fun in the moment."
""
"But it protected stability. And it protected the team’s focus."
""
"I’m learning that leadership is not just pushing for progress. It’s also knowing when to slow down so progress does not turn into rework."
""
"How do you decide when to move fast, and when to wait?

In woodworking, buying a new tool can feel like progress."
""
"Sometimes it is. A better tool can absolutely make better work."
""
"But I’ve also learned this the hard way."
""
"A fancy tool does not fix a fuzzy plan."
""
"If I’m not clear on the cut I’m trying to make, I can spend a lot of money and still end up frustrated."
""
"Work is similar."
""
"In IT, when things feel messy, it’s tempting to add another platform."
"Another dashboard. Another monitor. Another system that promises to simplify everything."
""
"And sometimes new tooling is the right answer."
""
"But if we don’t have clarity on what problem we’re solving, we just add cost and complexity."
""
"A question I’m trying to ask more often before we add anything new:"
""
"What decision will this help us make, faster or better?"
""
"If the answer is not clear, we pause."
""
"What’s a tool or platform you stopped using because it added more complexity than value?

Ownership gets talked about a lot in IT."
""
"And if you’ve been on a team long enough, you’ve probably felt the bad version of it."
""
"Ownership that feels like blame."
"Ownership that means you’re on an island."
"Ownership that turns into “good luck, you’ve got it.”"
""
"That’s not what I mean."
""
"Lately I’ve been thinking about ownership in a different way."
""
"Not “who gets blamed when it breaks,” but “who makes sure it stays healthy.”"
""
"Ownership of an application."
"Ownership of a process."
"Ownership of a service that the business depends on."
""
"When ownership is clear, a few good things happen:"
"	•	Changes have a natural path, because someone knows the standards and the history."
"	•	Documentation stays alive, because someone actually feels responsible for keeping it current."
"	•	Monitoring improves, because someone notices patterns and tunes noise instead of living with it."
"	•	Vendor asks stop bouncing around, because there’s a clear front door for questions and decisions."
""
"It’s not about control. It’s about care and consistency."
""
"And it’s not a one-person job. The owner is the coordinator and the accountable voice. The team still supports, contributes, and cross-trains."
""
"But without an owner, “everyone owns it” quietly turns into “no one owns it.”"
""
"Where could clearer ownership help your team right now, a process, an application, or a tool?

Batch work is one of the simplest ways I know to make progress without burning out."
""
"In my shop, if I’m building multiple items, I don’t make one start to finish."
""
"I batch it."
""
"I cut all the pieces."
"Then I drill."
"Then I sand."
"Then I finish."
""
"It feels less exciting in the moment, because you don’t get that quick “one is done” win."
""
"But it’s faster. It’s cleaner. And it reduces mistakes because your brain stays in the same mode for longer."
""
"Network work has its own version of batching."
""
"For example, instead of doing switchport changes one at a time all day, we can group them into a short daily block and knock them out with the same standard checks."
""
"Same idea with monitoring hygiene. We can set a weekly window to tune noisy alerts, remove dead monitors, and make sure new sites and circuits are actually covered."
""
"Even documentation gets easier when you batch it. Pick one system each week, update the runbook while the details are fresh, and move on."
""
"None of that eliminates urgent work. Incidents still happen."
""
"But batching gives the team a way to stay proactive, reduce context switching, and keep the backlog from feeling endless."
""
"Where could batching help your team right now, even in a small way?

A small thing that’s helped my team more than I expected is getting specific about what “done” means."
""
"Because in IT, “done” can mean a lot of things."
""
"The system is back up."
"The change went in."
"The ticket stopped screaming."
""
"And when you’re busy, it’s really tempting to close it out and move on. I get it. There is always another thing waiting."
""
"But I’ve learned that “done enough” is how you create future work for yourself."
""
"So we started asking a simple question before we call something finished:"
""
"Are we done, or did we just stop?"
""
"I’ve seen too many tickets where the fix worked, but nobody wrote down what they found. Two weeks later it happens again and we’re back to rediscovering the same thing."
""
"For us, “done” usually includes a few basics:"
"	•	We double-check the outcome, not just that the change applied."
"	•	We leave decent notes in the ticket, so the next person isn’t stuck guessing."
"	•	We sanity-check monitoring and alerts, because changes have a way of creating new blind spots."
"	•	We update the documentation with what we found and what we changed, while it’s still fresh in our heads."
""
"None of this is hard. It’s just the part you want to skip when the issue is finally quiet. That’s exactly why we try to do it anyway."
""
"Because if we skip this part, we don’t really save time. We just push the cost into the future, and it usually shows up as an escalation or a late night problem."
""
"I’m trying to treat “definition of done” as a way to protect the team, not as extra bureaucracy."
""
"What’s something you wish your team did more consistently before calling work “done”?
Great event with some fantastic people!

In woodworking, “measure twice, cut once” sounds simple."
""
"It’s also the hardest advice to follow when you’re excited to make progress."
""
"Because measuring feels like you’re not building anything. It feels slow. It feels like you’re delaying the real work."
""
"I can’t tell you how many times I’ve skipped that step, confident in my measuring and my memory, only to find out during assembly that a piece was too long, or worse, too short."
""
"Now I’m irritated. My flow is gone. And I’m much more likely to make another mistake because I’m trying to fix the first one while I’m still feeling it."
""
"Work has a version of that too."
""
"In IT, especially during busy weeks, it’s tempting to rush a change because the solution feels obvious. You want to get it done and move on."
""
"But I’ve learned that a little “measure twice” time saves a lot of “cut twice” pain."
""
"For us, that looks like:"
"	•	A quick peer review, even when it feels unnecessary"
"	•	A clear rollback plan, even when you “won’t need it”"
"	•	Confirming dependencies before the change window starts"
"	•	Writing the stakeholder update before you hit deploy, so you’re not scrambling mid-change"
""
"None of that is complicated. It’s just discipline."
""
"And it’s one of the best ways I know to protect the team’s time and confidence."
""
"What’s your version of “measure twice” at work?

We were getting ready to push a routing configuration update to every office switch."
""
"We had tested it in the lab. We felt good about the underlying design. Confidence was high."
""
"The part that almost got us wasn’t the routing logic."
""
"It was a subnet mask typo in the deployment script."
""
"The engineer driving the change wasn’t sold on the peer review step. He felt like it would slow things down. And honestly, he also felt like, “I know what I’m doing.” The process felt to him like a signal that management didn’t trust the engineers."
""
"He submitted it anyway."
""
"The reviewing engineer pulled the script and caught it right away."
""
"Here’s the scary part."
""
"The config would have worked when deployed. Nothing would have immediately failed. We probably would have celebrated and moved on."
""
"But that one mask value would have created future routing conflicts because it would have made our address space look like it overlapped between sites. The kind of problem that shows up later, under pressure, and takes way longer to unwind."
""
"That review didn’t just catch a typo."
""
"It protected the future."
""
"It reminded me that fundamentals are not busywork. They’re how you keep today’s change from becoming next month’s incident."
""
"What’s one “simple discipline” your team uses that saves you from problems you never have to see?

In my shop, I have a bin of scrap wood that I keep “just in case.”"
""
"And every so often I have to be honest with myself:"
""
"Some of it isn’t scrap. It’s clutter."
""
"It has potential."
"It’s a good species."
"It’s not that small."
"And I can absolutely imagine the project I might use it for someday."
""
"But if I haven’t touched it in a year… I’m probably not going to."
""
"Keeping it doesn’t make me more prepared. It just makes it harder to find the pieces I actually use."
""
"We had a similar moment at work recently."
""
"We made the call to eliminate one of our monitoring applications. It had a lot of potential, but it was expensive, and we just never made the time to use it the way it deserved. On top of that, many of the features were already being handled by other tools."
""
"So we asked the uncomfortable question: are we keeping this because it’s helping us… or because we feel bad letting go of something with “potential”?"
""
"Letting it go wasn’t a failure. It was a choice to simplify, reduce overlap, and focus on what we’re actually going to maintain and use well."
""
"Sometimes the most responsible thing you can do is stop carrying the things you’re not going to invest in."
""
"What have you “kept around” because it had potential — and what helped you finally decide to either commit to it or let it go?

One of the biggest changes I’ve made as a manager isn’t about how we troubleshoot."
""
"It’s about how we communicate while we troubleshoot."
""
"Earlier in my career, I thought updates had to include the answer. If I didn’t have the fix yet, I felt like I didn’t have anything useful to say."
""
"Now I think the most helpful updates are often the simplest:"
"	•	Here’s what we know."
"	•	Here’s what we’ve tried."
"	•	Here’s what we’re doing next."
"	•	Here’s when you’ll hear from us again."
""
"That’s it."
""
"It doesn’t solve the problem by itself — but it reduces anxiety. It builds trust. And it gives the team room to work instead of constantly switching contexts to respond to pings."
""
"I’ve learned that when people ask for updates, they’re usually not trying to be difficult. They’re trying to understand risk and make decisions. A steady cadence helps everyone breathe."
""
"What does a “good update” look like on your team when you’re still in the middle of it?

In woodworking, a jig can feel like a detour."
""
"You want to jump straight into building the thing. But instead, you spend time making a guide, a stop block, a template… something that doesn’t look like progress at all."
""
"Until you use it."
""
"The jig doesn’t just make the cut faster."
"It makes the cut consistent."
""
"And that consistency does something underrated: it reduces stress. You’re not reinventing the process every time. You’re not second-guessing. You can focus on quality instead of worrying about whether you lined everything up perfectly."
""
"I think a lot of our work benefits from the same idea."
""
"A standard checklist for changes."
"A runbook that’s actually readable."
"A handoff template that captures the basics."
"A consistent way to communicate during incidents."
""
"None of it is exciting. But it builds “repeatable calm” — especially when the pace picks up."
""
"What’s one template, checklist, or standard your team has that quietly makes everything smoother?

“Jump in and help” is one of the best instincts on a good team."
""
"And sometimes… it’s also how things get messy."
""
"I’ve seen days where everyone is doing the right thing:"
"Someone is digging into an incident."
"Someone else is chasing down a circuit issue."
"Another person is deep in a change window."
"And a few more “quick asks” show up that aren’t quick at all."
""
"All helpful. All important."
""
"But when we’re not aligned, it turns into parallel work, duplicated work, and mixed signals. Then we’re not just solving problems — we’re also trying to untangle who’s doing what, what’s already been tried, and what’s still outstanding."
""
"One change that’s helped us is simple: a short morning stand-up."
""
"Not a long meeting. Just enough to get on the same page:"
""
"What are you working today?"
"Where are you blocked?"
"What’s time-sensitive?"
"What should the rest of the team avoid duplicating?"
""
"It doesn’t eliminate surprises (IT will always be IT). But it gives the day a little structure. And it keeps “helpful” from turning into “noisy.”"
""
"Do you have a stand-up (or something like it)? If so, what makes it actually useful instead of just another meeting?

In woodworking, the last 10% is the part that tests your patience."
"The project is assembled. It “works.” It even looks pretty good from a few feet away."
"But then comes the sanding. Breaking the edges. Applying the finish. Those little touch-ups you only notice when you slow down and really look."
"That’s the part that turns the piece into something you’re proud to put your name on."
""
"Work is a lot like that."
""
"In IT, we often celebrate the moment the system is back up, the change is complete, or the new service is live. And we should. That’s real progress."
""
"But that last 10% is where reliability lives:"
" Documentation that makes sense to someone who wasn’t in the room."
" Monitoring that catches the problem before a user does."
" Clean handoffs so the next person isn’t guessing."
" A quick post-incident note that helps future us."
""
"It’s not glamorous work. It doesn’t always show up on status reports. And it’s the first thing to get squeezed when everyone’s busy."
"But when we skip it, we pay for it later. Usually at the worst possible time."
"I’m trying to get better at treating finish work as part of the job, not extra credit."
""
"Where does the “last 10%” show up on your team, and what helps you protect time for it?

I used to think the fastest way through an outage was “all hands on deck.”"
""
"Sometimes it is."
"A lot of times… it’s not."
""
"We had a recent issue that wasn’t actually that complicated. It felt complicated because of the coordination (or lack of it)."
""
"Two engineers were troubleshooting in parallel, both trying to be helpful… but they weren’t sharing what they were doing."
"Three different conversation threads were going in Teams. "
"And people were checking in constantly, which I get, because everyone just wants to know, “Are we okay?”"
""
"At one point I realized we were spending more time stitching the story together than actually solving the problem."
""
"What we changed wasn’t adding a new tool or designing some perfect process."
""
"It was simply designating an owner and setting a cadence:"
""
"“Patrick’s driving. Everyone feed updates to him. We’ll do stakeholder updates every 15 minutes.”"
""
"The next issue went way better. The noise dropped, the team had room to think, and the updates felt steady instead of reactive."
""
"And the bigger win was the reminder I keep coming back to: clarity is a form of leadership - especially when things are stressful."
""
"What do you do to keep urgent work from turning into noisy work?

"One thing elephants do that really stuck with me is how they handle loss."
"When a member of the herd dies, they don’t rush past it. They pause. They gather. They acknowledge what was lost — together."

"Work doesn’t always give us space for that."
"Projects end. Teams change. People move on. And too often, we just jump straight to “what’s next” without recognizing what came before."

"Good leaders slow things down just enough to let people process change."
"Not to dwell — but to respect the moment."
""
"🐘 Acknowledge the work that was done."
"🐘 Recognize the people who carried it."
"🐘 Then help the team move forward with clarity and care."
""
"Change is easier when people feel seen through it."

Post 6/12 – Teach, Don’t Just Tell"
"Continuing the series: Lessons from the Elephant Matriarch"
""
"🐘 🐘 🐘 🐘🐘 🐘"
""
"Matriarchs ensure the next generation survives by teaching, not commanding."
""
"In teams, great leaders coach - they don’t just delegate. It's one thing to just hand off work to someone, it's another to take the time to explain the why of the ask, and to give feedback along the way."
""
"They turn mistakes into lessons and experience into systems."

Post 1/12 – Why the Elephant Matriarch Leads Better Than Most Managers"
""
"Lately, I’ve been thinking about what real leadership looks like - not the kind that’s written in books, but the kind that actually holds a team together."
""
"That reflection led me down an unexpected path: elephants."
"More specifically, the elephant matriarch — the calm, experienced leader at the heart of every herd."
""
"I spent time researching how matriarchs lead, protect, and teach their herds, and the parallels to managing a high-performing team were remarkable."
"So, over the next few weeks, I’ll be sharing a 12-part series: “Lessons from the Elephant Matriarch.”"
"Each post will explore one behavior and how it translates into better leadership for teams like ours."
""
"🐘  🐘🐘🐘🐘🐘"
"When an elephant herd moves, every member — from calf to elder — follows the matriarch."
""
"Not because she demands it."
"But because she’s earned it."
""
"She remembers every watering hole, every dry season, every safe path. Her authority isn’t positional — it’s earned through wisdom, composure, and care."
""
"We often think leadership is about having answers. But real leadership looks more like the matriarch:"
""
"🐘 Steady in crisis"
"🐘 Patient in growth"
"🐘 Trusted through experience"
""
"Great leaders don’t control their teams."
"They guide them — calmly, consistently, and with confidence built on years of knowing the terrain."
""
"👉 Who has been the “matriarch” in your career — the person whose steadiness made everyone better?"
""
"#Leadership #TeamCulture #Management #WisdomInAction #ElephantLeadership

We spend so much time developing technical skills that soft skills sometimes get pushed aside."
""
"But I’ve seen communication, empathy, and collaboration make or break a project just as often as configuration errors."
""
"If you could wave a wand and instantly strengthen one soft skill across your engineering team, what would it be — and why?"
""
"#Leadership #Engineering #ITManagement #TeamCulture #Communication  #SRE #CareerGrowth

Taste the donuts."
""
"My son was watching a video this week about a very successful bakery. The owner was explaining how she spends a lot of time doing research trying to create new danishes and rolls that they can add to the menu. "
""
"She then said that an equal amount of attention was paid to their existing lineup. They taste each batch, verifying that they are up to snuff, and then discuss ideas of tweaks they can make in an effort to improve on their offerings, including their best selling donuts."
""
"My IT mind immediately equated this to process improvement. We spend a lot of time working on new things but it’s really easy to take the existing processes for granted, ESPECIALLY if they’re working."
""
"Just because the process is working doesn't mean there's not room for improvement. We need to make time to make sure what we’re doing is still the right thing. Or done in the right way. "
""
"We need to taste the donuts.

When I first became a manager, I treated one-on-ones like status meetings. I came in with an agenda, asked about projects, and wrapped up in 20 minutes."
""
"One day an engineer started the meeting with, “I’m burned out.” That was not on my list, but it changed the whole conversation. We talked about workload, balance, and how they could get back on track."
""
"That meeting opened my eyes. One-on-ones are not about tasks, they are about people. Now I let the team member lead. If they want to talk about a project, great. If they want to talk about their goals, even better. If they just need to vent, I listen."
""
"The surprising part is that I get better updates on projects too. People open up when they feel heard. My job in those meetings is to listen more than I talk."
""
"One-on-ones used to be my least favorite meetings. Now they are the ones I look forward to most.

When I first became a manager, I thought my reputation would come from how well I could present to leadership or how quickly I made decisions during incidents. I’ve since learned something much more important: your reputation is tied directly to your team’s success."
""
"If your team is thriving (projects finishing smoothly, incidents handled quickly, and people growing in their careers) everyone notices. If they’re struggling, people notice that too."
""
"That’s why I’ve shifted my focus from “What am I achieving?” to “How do I create conditions where my team succeeds?” That means:"
"	•	Clearing the roadblocks that get in their way"
"	•	Making sure they have tools that actually help, not just add noise"
"	•	Giving them trust and ownership, instead of micromanagement"
""
"When they succeed, I succeed. When they grow, I grow. And when they feel supported, they produce results that speak louder than anything I could ever put on a resume."
""
"The truth is, your reputation isn’t built in meetings. It’s built in the day-to-day wins of the people you lead.

Out with the old, in with the new. "
"My amazing team did what everyone said couldn’t be done. They migrated over 14,000 users around the world from a Cisco UC phone system over to Microsoft Teams Phone in under 3 months. We also implemented a “Softphone first” philosophy for everyone but our Executive Assistants and Front Desk personnel. I am so proud of them and everyone else across our IT organization that played a part in this project.

When people think of IT expertise, they picture deep technical skills: routing protocols, security policies, scripting automation."
""
"Those matter, but the skill I think is most underestimated? Translation."
""
"The ability to take complex technical concepts and explain them clearly to someone without a technical background is huge. It’s how you get buy-in for projects, avoid misunderstandings, and ensure stakeholders trust your recommendations."
""
"I’ve seen technically brilliant engineers struggle because they couldn’t communicate their ideas outside of their peer group. I’ve also seen people with solid - but not extraordinary - technical skills become invaluable because they could bridge that communication gap."
""
"Translation is about more than just simplifying. It’s about making the information relevant to the listener. An exec doesn’t need a deep dive into packet flow; they need to understand the business impact. A front line employee doesn’t need a security policy’s legal text; they need to know how it affects their day-to-day."
""
"If you want to stand out in IT, sharpen your communication skills. The better you translate, the more people will listen.

The big outages get attention. The “all hands on deck” moments make stories. But the truth is, the work that prevents those moments is the real hero of IT."
""
"Preventative maintenance isn’t flashy. It’s firmware updates, cable checks, patching, config cleanups, and running tests no one will ever hear about - because they prevent problems before they happen."
""
"We recently had a maintenance window where the only outcome was… nothing happened. No downtime. No alarms. Just a quiet, stable network. And honestly, that’s my favorite kind of success."
""
"The tricky part is that preventative work is easy to de-prioritize. There’s no red blinking alert demanding your attention. But skip it for too long, and those “small” issues turn into full-blown outages."
""
"So here’s to the engineers quietly keeping the lights on. You might not get a round of applause every week, but your work is the reason the crisis calls are rare.

There’s a special kind of panic that comes when you need a configuration detail, a vendor contact, or an obscure command syntax… and you realize the only person who knew it left the company three months ago."
""
"Documentation is one of those things that feels optional when everything’s going smoothly, but becomes absolutely critical the moment something goes wrong."
""
"I’ve learned to think of documentation like an insurance policy. You hope you never need it urgently — but when you do, you’ll be grateful it’s there."
""
"Some things that help:"
"- Write things down while they’re fresh"
"- Include screenshots or diagrams for tricky processes"
"- Keep a central, searchable location so nothing gets lost in email threads"
""
"The trick is to write for "future you". Not the “you” that just spent all week working on this project and knows every detail, but the “you” six months from now who’s dealing with an unrelated issue and needs a quick answer at 2 a.m."
""
"If you’ve ever cursed yourself for not documenting something, make today the day you start."
"

Managing a network team reminds me a lot of running a Dungeons & Dragons campaign."
""
"You’ve got different “classes” — the stealthy problem-solver who quietly fixes issues before anyone notices, the tank who jumps in on the hardest problems, the wizard with deep knowledge of arcane systems and acronyms."
""
"Every project is a quest. Every outage is a monster to fight. And every successful deployment is a shared victory."
""
"The magic happens when everyone understands their role and trusts the others to play theirs. Just like in D&D, no one person can carry the whole thing. The “party” succeeds because of collaboration, shared goals, and the occasional lucky roll (or in IT terms, finding that one obscure KB article at just the right moment)."
""
"And just like in a good campaign, there’s always another adventure around the corner — a new technology to explore, a migration to plan, a network to optimize."
""
"Sometimes we win big. Sometimes we limp back to the tavern to regroup. Either way, we keep playing."
"
Sharing for visibility. I worked with Joe for a number of years and he’s an amazing engineer.

If it’s not broken, you can still fix it."
""
"A lot of improvements don’t come from outages or tickets. They come from someone on the team saying:"
""
"“Hey... could this be easier?”"
""
"That kind of thinking saves hours later. And you don’t need an alert to tell you it’s worth doing.

I don’t need my team to know everything. I just need them to ask great questions."
""
"Certs are cool. Deep knowledge is great. But curiosity? That’s what moves the needle."
""
"If you’ve ever asked:"
"“What happens if we try it this way?”"
"“Why are we doing it like that?”"
"“Is that the only way?”"
""
"...you’re my kind of teammate.

"It’s a small change" is how half of my gray hairs were born."
"That sentence should come with a warning label. Especially in networking."
""
"One wrong VLAN. One mistyped IP. One checkbox you didn’t notice. Boom - half the company can’t connect to anything."
""
"The lesson? Small changes still need big attention."
"And maybe a second set of eyes.

Listening might be the most underrated skill in tech leadership."
""
"A few months into managing the Network team at HDR, I realized the best thing I could do was simple: just listen."
""
"Not to the systems. Not to the alerts. To the people."
""
"Some teammates needed direction. Others needed space. A few just needed someone they could count on when things got messy."
""
"No fancy dashboards. Just a notebook, a lot of one-on-ones, and a genuine effort to understand what makes each person tick."
""
"Turns out, building trust isn’t complicated. It’s just human.

"8-11am Training. I’m currently working on a PMP certification."
"11am-1pm Eat Lunch and read"
"1pm-2pm More job-hunting stuff. Circle back on jobs I've applied for and update my tracking spreadsheets. "

The company went through so many changes while I was there. They were a startup when I started. Then they were publicly traded. They went through bankruptcy, went private, were acquired several times, and finally emerged as a stronger, more solid, more mature company that was making acquisitions of their own. "
"Change is inevitable. And with change comes new opportunities for growth. I am both a little scared and excited to see what the future has in store for me. "
